title: Life expectancy, including UN projections
subtitle: The [period life expectancy](#dod:period-life-expectancy) at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.
caption:
Life expectancy, including UN projections
The period life expectancy at birth. This includes the observed life expectancy since 1950, and the medium-variant projections for the future, based on estimates by the UN Population Division.

The visualization tracks period life expectancy at birth from 1950 through 2100, combining historical observations with medium-variant projections. The vertical axis measures age in years from 0 to 100, while the horizontal axis spans the timeline. Five distinct series represent Japan, Sweden, Brazil, India, and the global average. In 1950, the data shows a wide disparity: Japan and Sweden begin in the 60 to 70-year range, whereas India starts near 40 years and Brazil near 50 years. The lines for Japan and Sweden show a steady upward trajectory, reaching approximately 85 years by 2020. The line for India displays a steeper rate of increase over the observed period, rising from the lowest position in 1950 to roughly 70 years by 2020. A visible dip occurs in the India series around 2020, followed by a recovery. The World average line sits between the high-income and emerging-economy groups, climbing from roughly 48 years in 1950 to over 70 years in 2020. Beyond the observed data, the dotted projection lines extend to 2100, showing continued growth for all groups. The projections place Japan near 95 years and Sweden near 90 years by the end of the century. Brazil and the World average are projected to reach the mid-80s, while India is projected to approach 85 years. The gap between the highest and lowest values narrows significantly over the full timeline as the lower-starting countries show faster rates of increase.
